sample_rows.Description: Low purchasing power of citizens and high share of low-income pensioners; Weak financial capacity of local self-government units; Lack of developed and tested financing models for renovation investments other than grants; Long return on investment due to low energy prices and extensive use of firewood and high construction costs; Necessity of structural renovation of buildings (and buildings damaged by earthquakes, for putting them into service and intact buildings, to ensure the safety of the use of the building) which cannot be covered by energy savings

Shortage of skilled labour – a key barrier that partly generates both financial recommendations (high labour costs) and contributes to slower renovation dynamics as well as potential quality issues of the works carried out

Complex procedures for using land owned by local self-government units and obtaining the necessary consent (in built-up urban areas, often the physical space in the immediate vicinity of the building, needed to house the system, is not private property and requires a complex procedure for obtaining prior consent for exploration works (where needed) and consent for land servitude); Lengthy approval procedures for grid connection (increase of connection power for heat pumps, connection of photovoltaic power plants to the distribution network); Lengthy public procurement processes in the public sector, often with unsatisfactory results; Unresolved property-law relationships for buildings; Complexity of the multidisciplinary approach to renovation

sample_rows.Capacity: 788 natural persons and 500 legal persons (in addition to the required engineering and professional training to obtain the authorisation); Compulsory professional training for authorised certifiers every two years

13.759 members of the professional chambers in construction (architectural, construction, electrical engineering and mechanical engineering); 27.320 persons employed in architectural and engineering activities in legal persons (2022); Among active professionals with more than 10 years of experience, existing knowledge and skills show an understanding of nZEB principles in medium-level system design while in execution, design, realisation, quality assurance and performance monitoring, as well as BIM skills is below medium-level; The professional development of authorised engineers is mandatory in the area of monitoring the development of the profession and the acquisition of new knowledge and skills

In 2024, there were 11.600 craftsmen in construction, with 28.378 employees in the first Q4 2025; As of March 2025, there were 21.202 active legal persons in construction with 150.813 employed; The value of works carried out by construction site workers in 2023 is 5.48 billion EUR and is 35.7% higher than in 2022; 13.188 or 62.20% of the enterprises in construction involved in energy renovations are small enterprises with one to nine employees; 5.511 or 26% are enterprises without employees; companies with more than 50 employees account for a total of 1.5% of active legal persons

sample_rows.Resulting From: Overlaps among housing, energy, and social policies without a unified renovation roadmap.

sample_rows.Barrier: Lack of interest on the part of building decision-makers in financial instruments
sample_rows.Resulting From: Grant culture with 100% grants in prior renovation programs discourages use of financial instruments
No detailed segmentation by type, age, and performance of single-family homes.

sample_rows.Barrier: Complexity of decision-making for renovation and debt financing of multi-family residential buildings
sample_rows.Resulting From: Decisions regarding modernizations require the approval of 2/3 of all owners. Bank loans for renovation require the written agreement of all owners

sample_rows.Barrier: Lack of interest on the part of building decision-makers in financial instruments
sample_rows.Resulting From: Grant culture with 100% grants in prior renovation programs discourages use of financial instruments

sample_rows.Barrier: Limited opportunities for participation of homeowners in selection of measures and management/control of program implementation
sample_rows.Resulting From: Renovation program procedures do not provide opportunities for homeowners to participate in management of renovation projects

sample_rows.Barrier: Lack of interest on the part of building decision-makers in financial instruments
sample_rows.Resulting From: Grant culture with 100% grants in prior renovation programs discourages use of financial instruments
Lack of multi-year budgeting, restrictions of retention of cost savings, limitations on using operating savings for capital expenses
Lengthy approval chains and rigid procurement procedures as well as requirements for lowest up-front cost are unfavorable to energy-efficient renovation projects

sample_rows.Barrier: Lack of interest on the part of building decision-makers in financial instruments
sample_rows.Resulting From: Grant culture with 100% grants in prior renovation programs discourages use of financial instruments
Limits the interest of commercial building owners in renovation

sample_rows.Barrier: Lack of incentives or technical assistance
sample_rows.Resulting From: There are few incentives or technical assistance programs to help energy auditing, identification of energy efficiency opportunities, and preparation of financing applications

sample_rows.﻿Instrument: Law 372/2005 on Energy Performance of Buildings (consolidated 2024)
sample_rows.Scope: Transposes EPBD: EPC regime, NZEB/new-build requirements, inspections, auditor accreditation and sanctions; EPC as compliance evidence.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Mc 001-2022; EPC templates (MDLPA 2023); Order 2237/2010; QA sampling under MDLPA & ISC; official PDF (ISC).

sample_rows.Scope: Defines asset-rating methodology for EPCs, renovation assessment, RES integration and documentation deliverables.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Monitorial Oficial 46/17.01.2023; MDLPA publication; professional training notices.

sample_rows.﻿Instrument: Order 2237/2010 on energy auditor accreditation (as amended)
sample_rows.Scope: Grades, competence, renewal and QA controls for building energy auditors.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: MDLPA accreditation portal; national EPC registry; QA sampling protocols.

sample_rows.Scope: Municipal programs for renovating multi-apartment buildings; roles of municipalities/HOAs; procedural steps and documentation. Operates as a main delivery chain for worst performance residential blocks under the MEPS roadmap (Section 3.3).
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Norms (Order 163/2009); municipal program guides; periodic updates (2010–2023).

sample_rows.﻿Instrument: OUG 69/2010 + HG 736/2010 norms (repealed by OUG 52 of 2025)
sample_rows.Scope: Government-guaranteed loans for thermal rehabilitation; complements grant schemes under PNRR/AFM. Closes affordability gaps, especially for HOAs.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Norms via HG 736/2010; annual guarantee ceilings through MoF.

sample_rows.Scope: Deep/moderate renovation of public and residential stock; includes registries/logbooks and BRP pilots; links with seismic strengthening where needed. Supports early MEPS cohorts and ZEB ready solutions. I1.1 Integrated renovation of apartment buildings; I1.2 Energy renovation of apartment buildings in disadvantaged areas; I1.3 Energy renovation of residential buildings; I.1.4 Integrated renovation of public buildings; I.1.5 Energy renovation of public buildings; I2. Creation of the National Register of Buildings; I4. Circular economy and increasing the energy efficiency of historic buildings.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: MDLPA C5 guidance, calls & clarifications; MIS monitoring; cross-references to NECP and MEPS readiness.

sample_rows.Scope: Completes prosumer/RES framework (individual & collective prosumers; settlement, connection, balancing). Key enabler for Article 10 solar obligations and electrification in MEPS packages.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: ANRE Orders 15/2022 & 95/2022; subsequent adjustments; public consultations (2024–2025).

sample_rows.Scope: Grants for residential PV + (2024) mandatory storage; standardized rules and accredited installers. Aligned with MEPS triggers for roof-level works.

BACS & SRI options; obligations for large non-residential heating/cooling power; building automation & control by thresholds. Provides an additional path to compliance for MEPS and improved monitoring.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: EU Delegated 2020/2155 and Implementing 2020/2156 on SRI; national adoption pathway (planned).

sample_rows.Scope: Ducting/pre-cabling and charging points at new/renovated buildings; streamlining in co-ownerships; fire-safety & accessibility requirements. Integration with building permits and technical regulations.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Several provisions were already introduced in the legislation.

sample_rows.Scope: Regulates public DH service; facilitates rehabilitation and efficient DH trajectories consistent with EED Art. 26; supports low-temperature operation and decarbonized heat as a compliant MEPS pathway for connected buildings.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Municipal strategies; Modernization Fund and state aid schemes (REPowerEU).

sample_rows.﻿Instrument: OUG 92/2024 — National network of One-stop shops (OSS)
sample_rows.Scope: County/municipal OSS counters and a national portal; prioritizes vulnerable consumers; streamlines access to finance and permits.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Implementation via MDLPA guidance and local decisions; links to PNRR.

sample_rows.Scope: Horizontal EE framework (targets, programs, public-sector leadership, audits and planning). Provides coherence with EED and NECP trajectories.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: National EE programs; NECP integration; reporting.

sample_rows.Scope: Bans public incentives for new stand-alone fossil-fuel boilers (limited exceptions); aligns funding with decarbonization and MEPS.
sample_rows.Key Implementing Documents / Authorities: Applies across AFM/PNRR/local programs; implementation guidance to authorities.

sample_rows.﻿Instrument (official title): Legea nr. 372/2005 privind performanța energetică a clădirilor (consolidată 2024)
sample_rows.Scope & Principles: Backbone of EPB in Romania: EPC regime, requirements for new buildings (NZEB), documentation and inspections; basis for MEPS compliance through EPC evidence.
sample_rows.Implementing Documents: Metodologia Mc 001-2022; modele EPC (MDLPA 03.03.2023); Ordin 2237/2010 – acreditare; proceduri QA.

sample_rows.﻿Instrument (official title): Metodologia Mc 001-2022 (OMDLPA 16/2023) – Metodologie de calcul al performanței energetice a clădirilor
sample_rows.Scope & Principles: Defines EPB calculation, integration of systems/RES, EPC outputs and documentation; mandatory for EPC issuance and compliance checks.
sample_rows.Implementing Documents: Monitorul Oficial 46/17.01.2023; MDLPA publication; EPC templates.

sample_rows.﻿Instrument (official title): Ordin nr. 2237/2010 – Regulament privind atestarea auditorilor energetici pentru clădiri
sample_rows.Scope & Principles: Defines grades, competencies, practice renewal and QA; ensures EPC integrity.
sample_rows.Implementing Documents: Publicat în M.Of. nr. 683/08.10.2010; actualizări ulterioare.

sample_rows.Description of Risk: Limited availability of qualified contractors and materials, especially for heat pumps, insulation, windows, BACS, and PV; risk of cost inflation and delays.
sample_rows.Mitigation Measures: Medium-term skills and certification programmes; targeted training for key trades; framework contracts; advance procurement planning; market intelligence monitoring.

sample_rows.Description of Risk: Municipalities and homeowner associations may lack the expertise and staff needed to manage MEPS-scale renovation programmes, procurement, and oversight.
sample_rows.Mitigation Measures: OSS staffing and training; standardized templates for audits, design, procurement and HOA decision-making; technical helpdesks; dedicated support teams for complex HOAs.

sample_rows.Description of Risk: Lengthy permitting processes and slow DSO response times for PV and heat pump connections, especially under Article 10 solar-triggered works.
sample_rows.Mitigation Measures: Streamlined digital permitting workflows; single-entry systems; accelerated DSO/TSO coordination; fast-track grid connection procedures for MEPS-triggered installations; clear national service-level agreements.

sample_rows.EPB-Aligned Evidence to Retain: As-is EPB model; EPC baseline; constraints (heritage, usage, grid/DH context).
Fabric/system/RES combinations that can meet mandatory EPB checks; Article-10 applicability confirmed.
Mc 001-2022 model + SR EN 15459-1 global cost; include residual values and mandatory financial parameters.

sample_rows.Design: EPC demonstrating achievement of the threshold by the deadline; Renovation Passport acceptable for staged compliance with dated milestones.
County dashboards; EPC analytics by tenure, income decile and climate zone; OSS tracking of TA cases and completions.
Graduated approach prioritizing support and resolution; possible fines for systematic refusal; conditionality on access to public benefits/subsidies; enforcement focused on repeat non-compliance.

sample_rows.Application: Adjust deadlines for documented technical infeasibility or disproportionate cost; require an alternative package or staged plan.
Higher grant rates; turnkey delivery via OSS; protected timelines; prohibition of eviction on grounds of compliance-related works.
Adaptive scheduling windows if proven market bottlenecks occur; framework contracts to stabilize prices.

headers: Illustrative Outcome (to be populated with modelling)
non_time_columns: Illustrative Outcome (to be populated with modelling)

sample_rows.Illustrative Outcome (to be populated wi: kWh/m² reduction and TWh savings nationally for each MEPS phase; improved load factors via BACS and better envelopes.
MtCO₂ eq cumulative by 2030/2035/2040 based on EPC conversions and fuel-switch; alignment with NECP and EED annual savings obligations.
Number of vulnerable households upgraded; reduction in energy-burden %; improved comfort and IAQ.

sample_rows.Action: Adopt secondary legislation defining non-residential thresholds, coverage, deadlines, and exemptions/safeguards; establish governance and QA rules. Develop and Launch MEPS portal, OSS scale-up, and first funding window targeted at worst-performers; begin pre-notifications. Phase-in non-residential MEPS Deadline 1; monitor compliance and adjust enabling finance.
Phase-in Deadline 2 for non-residential; adopt residential trajectory design and residential MEPS thresholds.
Tighten thresholds in line with national trajectory, Article 10 solar roll-out, and district heating decarbonization under EED Art. 26.

sample_rows.﻿Policy / Measure: Non-residential MEPS — Phase 1 (worst cohort to minimum class F)
sample_rows.Expected Energy & GHG Impact: High kWh/m² savings from bottom cohort; early MtCO₂ cuts via electrification/DH + Article-10 PV at roof works.
sample_rows.KPIs (annual): % of cohort achieving threshold; TWh saved; MtCO₂ cut; EPC class uplift distribution; PV capacity installed (MWp); Share with BACS/controls
sample_rows.Indicative Funding / Enablers: AFM (EE/RES lines), PNRR/REPowerEU windows, stated for SMEs/public, EIB frameworks; OSS TA vouchers.
sample_rows.Compliance / Notes: EPC-based compliance; narrow exemptions; QA sampling; penalties per Art. 9(7).

sample_rows.Expected Energy & GHG Impact: Additional TWh savings; deeper GHG reductions; market learning reduces costs.
sample_rows.KPIs (annual): Compliance rate vs deadline; Incremental TWh/MtCO₂; Audit pass rate; Cost per m²/kWh saved
sample_rows.Indicative Funding / Enablers: Continuation of Phase-1 lines; tax allowances; ESCO models; green leases for split incentives.
sample_rows.Compliance / Notes: Refine segmentation; maintain safeguards; publish compliance dashboards.

sample_rows.Expected Energy & GHG Impact: Largest energy poverty relief; significant heat demand cuts; MtCO₂ reductions in gas/biomass areas.
sample_rows.KPIs (annual): Dwellings upgraded (no.); TWh saved & MtCO₂; Vulnerable households assisted (no.); Average bill reduction (RON/yr)
sample_rows.Indicative Funding / Enablers: AFM Social/Heat & Solar Social, HOA lines with guarantees, municipal co-financing; OSS turnkey delivery.
sample_rows.Compliance / Notes: No disproportionate exemptions for rentals; RP (passport) accepted for staged compliance.

sample_rows.Relevant Provision(s) for MEPS: Article 9(1) (non-residential MEPS), Article 9(2) (residential progressive trajectory), Article 17(19) (safeguards), Articles 13, 23–24 (TBS & inspections), Article 10 (solar), Article 15 (SRI).
Articles 25–26: comprehensive heating/cooling assessments; efficient district heating & cooling; local heat planning (>45k inhabitants).
Articles 23–24: annual RES increase in H&C and DHC (links to enabling framework for worst-performers).

sample_rows.﻿Authority: Ministry of Development, Public Works and Administration (MDLPA)
sample_rows.Mandate and Responsibilities: Lead regulator; adopts secondary legislation and MEPS thresholds; defines compliance rules, exemptions and safeguards per Art. 17(19); oversees national EPC registry and QA.

sample_rows.﻿Authority: MDLPA, MIPE, Ministry of Energy / Environment Fund Administration (AFM)
sample_rows.Mandate and Responsibilities: Designs and disburses enabling finance aligned to MEPS milestones (worst-performers priority); ensures complementarity with RES/H&C funds.
Sets metering/billing rules, grid-connection simplifications, and BACS/SRI alignment where relevant; supports data access for monitoring.

sample_rows.Design: Cross-match EPC registry, local tax rolls, cadaster, commercial registries; flag worst-performer segments (e.g., EPC G/F non-residential) for notification.
MEPS portal with thresholds, deadlines, eligible measures, enabling finance, and FAQs; dashboard by county; communication in RO and EN.
Rolling letters/emails/SMS to identified owners 24–12–6 months before deadlines; OSS hotline and workshops.

sample_rows.Specification: Valid EPC issued under Mc 001-2022 demonstrating achievement of the MEPS threshold at or before the deadline.
Accredited asset model or metered-normalized performance indicator tied to a defined baseline and boundary; only for specified non-res sub-sectors; subject to QA.
Risk-based sampling; mandatory documentation pack (inputs, photos, invoices, commissioning); financial and juridical sanctions for material misstatements.

sample_rows.Design: Performance-based grants/loans prioritizing worst-performers; bonus for early compliance; tax allowances for SMEs; tailored state-aid lines for public bodies.
OSS and sectoral advisory (retail, hospitality, logistics); model specs; procurement frameworks; M&V guides.
Model green leases; cost-recovery via service charges (ESCO); standard clauses endorsed by Government.

sample_rows.﻿Monitoring: Quarterly dashboard (county & region & national); EPC registry analytics; control/verification sampling; publication of compliance rates.
sample_rows.Penalty Scheme (effective, proportionate: Administrative fines scaled to floor area and delay; escalating daily penalties after a grace period; conditional access to public funding; public naming for persistent non-compliance; mandatory corrective action plans; potential closure restrictions for safety-critical failures.

sample_rows.Design: The subset covered (i.e., EPC class G and F); include both owner-occupied and rented units — without disproportionate exemptions for rentals (Art. 9(2) fifth sub-paragraph).
Quantify the portion of the national trajectory met through MEPS vs. other instruments (grants, building code triggers).
Staged thresholds: G→F by 2028; G/F→E by 2030, aligned with funding and contractor capacity.

sample_rows.Design (with priority for vulnerable hou: Dedicated financial support lines for worst-performers; higher aid intensities for vulnerable and energy-poor households; performance-based tranches; guarantees for HOAs.
One-stop-shops; Renovation Passports to stage works; standardized HOA decision packs; turnkey pathways for vulnerable households (Heat/Solar Social).
Model cost-sharing in HOAs; landlord-tenant frameworks enabling rent caps in exchange for efficiency upgrades and comfort guarantees.

sample_rows.measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
sample_rows.measure_name: Measure 1 - Strengthen communication on renovation obligations and opportunities
sample_rows.description:
Faced with the challenges of legibility, fair access to information and
mobilisation of all renovation stakeholders in Wallonia, the measure aims to set
up a centralised communication dedicated to information and activation for
renovation. This unified communication must centralise content, make users’
journeys clear and consistent, and support the action of field relays. It targets
both the residential sector and the non-residential, private and public sector
(municipalities, CPAS, etc.), as well as professional actors in renovation.
This measure constitutes a transversal axis of the Renovation Plan, at the service
of the legibility of legislative and organizational reforms. Its objective is to
create a unified, proactive and continuous communication strategy, enabling
both:
•
Strengthen understanding of obligations;
•
encourage mobilisation towards support schemes;
•
Encourage changes in behaviour related to the energy use of housing.
Specifically, the measure provides for:
•
Harmonisation and updating of information on energy renovation in
Wallonia (financial support, support, EPB standards) on a unified
portal, interoperable with the sites of the SPW and the other
administrations concerned;
•
Providing accessibledecision support tools: decision tree pointing to
the right device, global housing cost simulator, plain language content;
•
Activation of key moments in the life of the building (purchase,
inheritance, rental) by means of communication kits and a proactive
strategy for professional actors (architects, contractors, etc.).
The strategy will combine the direct dissemination of information and its
increased visibility in the spaces frequented by the target audiences.
Finally, continuous campaigns will highlight the co-benefits of renovation
(comfort, health, heritage value, economies, climate), supported by credible
feedback to stimulate demand.
sample_rows.quantified_objectives:
- ≥ 50 % awareness of the device in 2029
- ≥ 200 000 visits/month to the portal.
- 100% of regional sites related to energy renovation
harmonised (financial support, accompaniments, EPB standards) in
2028
sample_rows.budget:
- Single portal and digital tools: €€€€
- Media campaigns and content: €€€
- Kits & animation of relays: €€ €€
sample_rows.stakeholders: SPW TLPE, one-stop shops, SWCS ACER actors

sample_rows.measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
sample_rows.measure_name: Measure 2 - Consolidate a centralised database on the energy performance of buildings
sample_rows.description:
Precise knowledge of the building stock is the condition for effectively steering
the renovation policy and ensuring compliance with the obligations set by the
European Directive EPB 2024/1275. However, in Wallonia, information on
buildings is now scattered among several registers (cadastre, EPB, audits,
permits, financial aid, municipal data), without interconnection or consolidated
vision. This fragmentation makes it impossible to reliably assess the state of
the building, the level of energy performance achieved and the monitoring of
renovations.
The consolidation of a centralised database addresses this strategic challenge.
This database will be linked to the building passport. It will make it possible to
identify all non-residential dwellings and buildings, to associate each of them
with a unique identifier and to link all available administrative, technical and
energy information to it. The automatic cross-breeding between EPB
certificates and rightholders in rem will ensure the traceability of the park and
will form the basis for the implementation and control of the renovation
obligations. This database will become the unique repository of the Walloon
building stock. It will prioritise the integration of EPB certificates, audits and
HVAC inspection reports.
The tool will enable the Region to ensure:
1. A comprehensive and dynamic view of the state of Walloon buildings
2. Automatic identification of uncertified or non-compliant units
3. Monitoring compliance with obligations and renovation trajectories
4. And a harmonised transmission of data to the European Commission
By ensuring consistency, security and continuous updating of data, this
database will be a key lever for energy planning, administrative transparency
and public policy impact assessment. Ultimately, it will allow Wallonia to have
a complete and dynamic energy mapping of buildings, a prerequisite for
achieving the decarbonisation of the park by 2050.
sample_rows.quantified_objectives:
- Implementation of a centralised database in 2028
- Set up automatic tracking of non-EPB units and their rightholders in
rem by 2032.
- Notify 100% of affected homeowners at least 3 years before the
renovation obligation deadlines.
sample_rows.budget:
- Technical design, interoperability and security €€€
- Management cost €€€

sample_rows.measure_category: Governing and steering the renovation strategy
sample_rows.measure_name: Measure 3 - Establish an observatory for renovation and building trades
sample_rows.description:
Wallonia has many sources of data on renovation and employment in the
building sector, but these are fragmented and incompletely exploited. While
the centralised database on the energy performance of buildings will provide
the technical basis for collection and harmonisation, there is currently a lack of
a tool capable of transforming this information into reliable indicators,
forward-looking analyses and strategic steering tools.
The EPBD 2024/1275 requires structured monitoring: renovation rate and
depth, actual performance, availability of skilled labour. The accumulated
delays in the objectification of renovations and occupations create a risk of
European non-compliance and reduce the credibility of Walloon policies. In
addition, the actors in the sector (companies, federations, training bodies) lack
visibility on future needs, which complicates planning.
The observatory will have a dual role:
1. Renovation: use data from the centralised database, permits, financial
support, audits, to measure the pace and depth of renovations, verify
the actual efficiency (before/after works) and monitor the impact of
regional policies.
2. On the trades: collect and cross-reference data from Statbel,
FOREM, IFAPME, Embuild, in order to identify critical jobs,
anticipate needs, adjust training policies and support sectors.
3. Materials: using the data from fiche measure 16 to monitor the
development of local and circular sectors (share of local materials,
available EPDs, rates of circular public procurement, technological
maturity).
The observatory will also be an instrument of transparency: a public scoreboard
will present key indicators to citizens, municipalities and businesses, while
more detailed reports will guide policy-makers.
sample_rows.quantified_objectives:
- Observatory operational in 2029, first report published in 2030
- Produce an indicator of the rate of deep renovation and the average
label of the park as early as 2028
- Tracking critical workforce and business needs
- Cover 80% of relevant administrative data in the analysis by 2030.
sample_rows.budget:
- Technical set-up of the observatory: €€
- Annual operation (analyses, publications, surveys): €
- Prospective studies (jobs, industrial sectors): €
- Communication and public scoreboard: €
sample_rows.stakeholders:
SPW, IWEPS, CEHD, FOREM and IFAPME, Embuild, Statbel, GRD,
communes and intercommunales

sample_rows.measure_name: Introduction of national minimum energy performance standards (MEPS)
sample_rows.content: Legislative introduction of mandatory minimum energy classes for the least performing segment of the non-residential building stock (class E by 2030; class D by 2033), including powers to update thresholds
sample_rows.amending_legislation: Energy Efficiency Act (EEOS) – new/amended provisions for CEFs and deadlines

sample_rows.measure_name: Legal setting of limit values and methodology for CEFs
sample_rows.content: Legal setting of primary energy consumption limits per type of non-residential building, according to existing regulations
sample_rows.amending_legislation: Regulation No RD-02-20-3 of 9.11.2022 – addition with an explicit link to CEFR

sample_rows.measure_name: Introduction of a simplified energy labelling system
sample_rows.content: Establish a simplified procedure to pre-define the energy class of non-residential buildings based on a limited set of input data in order to identify buildings below CEFR
sample_rows.amending_legislation: EEOS – legal basis for an energy label; Regulation No E-RD-04-2 of 16.12.2022 – new section on simplified labelling
sample_rows.sources_of_funding: Life and Competitiveness and Innovation in Enterprises projects

sample_rows.measure_name: Energy efficiency obligation scheme
sample_rows.short_description: Energy efficiency obligation scheme. Obligated parties – energy suppliers – implement measures contributing to decarbonisation of buildings (advanced metering, monitoring and optimisation of consumption, installation of photovoltaic systems, etc.) and purchase energy savings from third parties such as ESCO companies. Activities valued through administrative increase of up to 30% in savings achieved for energy poverty measures.
sample_rows.quantified_objective: Not specifically quantified in the measure table.
sample_rows.planned_budget_and_sources: Not specifically stated; sources: EU ETS revenues, supplier's fees.
sample_rows.expected_impacts: Contributing to building renovation, decarbonisation of buildings, and addressing energy poverty.
sample_rows.implementation_status: In implementation - referenced across multiple sections without dedicated table

sample_rows.measure_name: Promoting decarbonisation and applying the energy efficiency first principle in buildings
sample_rows.short_description: The measure builds on the Charter of Cooperation for the Decarbonisation of Buildings by 2050, strengthens inter-departmental cooperation between the public and private sectors through workshops and dialogues, focusing on the renovation of buildings and the construction of nZEB and ZEB performance, the reduction of emissions, the increase of RES and the uptake of smart technologies.
sample_rows.authorities_responsible: Ministry of Physical Planning Construction and State Assets (MPGI)
sample_rows.expected_impacts: Network of experts and partners, continued cooperation on building renovation strategy, transition to nZEB standard, energy efficiency first info-campaign.
sample_rows.implementation_status: In implementation. Updates required: extension of topics in line with EU guidelines – focus on the energy efficiency first principle, boosting energy and comprehensive renovation of buildings, new EPBD requirements (minimum energy performance standards, renovation trajectories for residential buildings, minimum energy performance, urban mobility, solar systems, use of RES, ZEB, etc.).

sample_rows.measure_name: Energy renovation programme for multi-apartment buildings
sample_rows.short_description: Scheme adopted by Decision of the Croatian Government for the period up to 2030 (NN No 143/21). It encompasses integral, deep and comprehensive renovation. Three models: reconstruction of intact, reconstruction of earthquake-damaged, support to citizens at risk of energy poverty. An incentive to renovate buildings according to nZEB and ZEB performance is also planned. Encourage energy and comprehensive renovation which, in addition to energy efficiency measures, fosters an increase in the earthquake-proofness of the building, safety in case of fire, healthy indoor climate conditions, application of green infrastructure, urban mobility, accessibility for persons with disabilities and reduced mobility, including the use of RES, connection to efficient centralised systems, etc. New fossil fuel systems are not encouraged.
sample_rows.quantified_objective: Restore 6.27 million m2 in total by 2030, averaging around 700,000 m2 per year. Savings in 2030: 1.87 PJ of energy; reducing CO2 emissions: 124.88 kt. Cumulative until 2030: 8.45 PJ and 565.87 ktCO2.
sample_rows.type_of_policy_or_measure: Financial measure; grants and financial instruments (loans)
sample_rows.planned_budget_and_sources: NRRP (39.8 million EUR), ERDF (89 million EUR), an additional 120 million EUR (RePowerEU), EPEEF resources. A total of over 54.5 million EUR for the reconstruction of earthquake-damaged.
sample_rows.authorities_responsible: MPGI – development and monitoring of implementation; monitoring: MINGO - Monitoring of realised impacts and reporting
sample_rows.expected_impacts: Reducing energy consumption in multi-apartment buildings, increasing RES, reducing CO2 emissions. Increasing resilience to earthquakes and fires.
sample_rows.implementation_status: In implementation. Updates required: developing models to help homeowners at risk of energy poverty; differentiation of support for worst performing buildings and for other buildings; energy communities and energy sharing.

sample_rows.measure_name: Repair construction energy efficiency requirements
sample_rows.description: When corrective measures are taken, they must be carried out in accordance with the energy efficiency requirements.

sample_rows.measure_name: Building automation
sample_rows.description: The Automation and Control System shall be installed above 290 kW in heating and air-conditioning systems by 2025 and in renovated buildings from 2021.

sample_rows.measure_name: Building automation
sample_rows.description: System requirements will be set for technical building systems in terms of overall energy performance, correct installation and appropriate dimensioning, adjustment and control. The requirements concern technical systems that replace and upgrade existing systems in the building and shall be applied in so far as they are technically, economically and functionally feasible (Government proposal due in 2020).

sample_rows.measure_name: Article 2.01.02:2016 Design and certification of the energy performance of buildings
sample_rows.objective: This technical regulation applies to the energy performance of heated residential and non-residential buildings (parts thereof, energy performance calculation etc.).
sample_rows.expected_impact: All new and renovated buildings shall have an assigned energy performance class

sample_rows.measure_name: Law No I-1240 'Construction Law of the Republic of Lithuania'
sample_rows.objective: Regulate new construction and renovation of buildings and specify general technical and energy efficiency requirements. It also specifies minimum energy performance requirements for buildings and defines the certification of energy performance of buildings, the need for an energy performance certificate shall be indicated.
sample_rows.source: Law of the Republic of Lithuania on Construction, Articles 11 and 51
sample_rows.quantitative_target: Specify minimum energy performance requirements for buildings and define the certification process
sample_rows.expected_impact: Description of the procedure to be followed

sample_rows.measure_name: Setting minimum energy performance standards for residential and non-residential buildings
sample_rows.objective: Set minimum energy performance standards in relevant legislation
sample_rows.type_of_policy_or_measure: National minimum energy performance standards and accompanying policy measures
sample_rows.expected_impact: The share of buildings renovated shall increase by at least 20% by 2030.

sample_rows.measure_name: Amendments to the rules on the energy performance of buildings (PURES update)
sample_rows.objective: Updating the Rules on Energy Efficiency in Buildings (PURES) in line with the Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD). Details are regulated by the DSEPS.

sample_rows.measure_name: Renovation of cultural heritage buildings and other specific groups
sample_rows.objective: Guidelines for targeted funding to be drawn up for comprehensive renovation of cultural heritage buildings. Analyses of the necessary additional or adapted funding for the deep renovation of cultural heritage buildings. This includes: an analysis of the energy renovations carried out in terms of energy savings, investment, financial construction, etc., the development of criteria for determining the eligible costs for the energy renovation of these groups of buildings and their integration into all support instruments, provision of cohesion funding tailored to these target groups (for example also in separate calls) and the Climate Fund. Setting-up of information points for deep renovation of cultural heritage buildings (energy and material efficiency). Update the Guidelines for the Renovation of Cultural Heritage Buildings and accordingly refurbishment of cultural inheritances take into account, mutatis mutandis, the measures set out in the DSEPS for improvement of energy effectiveness and results of the CDE under preparation (methodology and orientations for the placement of photovoltaic installations on facilities in settlement heritage areas and others).

sample_rows.measure_name: Energy Contracts (EPO) and related financial instruments
sample_rows.objective: Continue the development and implementation of existing financial instruments and funding models: design relevant financial instruments for EPO service providers (guarantees, recoupment of investment through energy efficiency service bills, financing through a combination of grants and private funds/financial instruments, including financing of technical project preparation, provision of concessional credit, implementation of the EPO in the context of public procurement), support development of EPOs suitable supporting measures; for example training, additional professional and technical assistance for project preparation, programme for provisioning quality of EPO projects, preparation of tools for extend the public sector instrument to other sectors, in particular housing; establish a mechanism to encourage the emergence of energy service companies, with particular attention to small and medium-sized enterprises.
sample_rows.entities_responsible: MOPE, MKRR, SMES; Eco Fund, SID Bank

sample_rows.measure_name: Implementation of minimum energy performance standards (MEPS) in the tertiary sector
sample_rows.objective: According to Article 9 of the EPBD, the main objective of this measure is to ensure that all non-residential buildings are below a certain maximum energy performance threshold. In addition, and in accordance with Article 5 of the EPBD, this measure also aims to ensure that minimum energy performance requirements are set for buildings or building units in order to achieve at least cost-optimal levels and, where appropriate, more stringent benchmarks, such as the requirements for nearly zero-energy buildings and zero-emission buildings.
sample_rows.planned_budget_and_sources: EUR 800.000,00 from the budget of the AGE.

sample_rows.measure_name: Regulatory framework for the promotion of energy renovation
sample_rows.objective: In accordance with Article 8 of the EPBD, the objective of this measure is to ensure that when buildings undergo major renovation, the energy performance of the building or the renovated part thereof is upgraded in order to meet minimum energy performance requirements, provided that it is technically, functionally and economically feasible.
sample_rows.planned_budget_and_sources: The actions described in this measure are carried out directly with the own resources of the competent administrations involved and do not entail additional expenditure for them.
sample_rows.entities_responsible: MIVAU, MITERD, IDAE, IETcc, CCAA and EELL

sample_rows.measure_name: Public aid for energy renovation
sample_rows.objective: In accordance with Art. 17 of the EPBD, the objective of this measure is to ensure that appropriate funding, support measures and other instruments are available from the public domain to address market barriers in order to deliver the necessary investments to transform its building stock into zero-emission buildings by 2050 at the latest.
sample_rows.planned_budget_and_sources: EUR 0.000,00 from the budget of the General State Administration and EUR 487.200.000,00 from the budgets of the Autonomous Communities.
